summary: Yegge uses embedded systems and video games (particularly Mario Kart) as a framework for understanding the nature of boundaries in any system, including our universe. He explains that the 'invisible wall' at the edge of a game world represents a one-way frontier where the embedded system's rules simply end—there is no 'other side' in any meaningful sense from within that system. The concept of 'undefined' in programming becomes a metaphor for what lies beyond any system's boundaries: not 'nothing,' but 'anything'—a realm where normal rules don't apply and unpredictable phenomena can occur. This leads to the philosophical observation that all systems are embedded systems, including our universe, which has implications for questions about what existed before the Big Bang or beyond the universe's edge.
key_points: Embedded systems have one-sided frontiers where the rules of the system simply stop—asking 'what's on the other side' is semantically nonsensical from within the system
The 'undefined' space beyond a system boundary is not 'nothing' but 'anything'—a Wand of Wonder where unpredictable phenomena occur when programs accidentally cross it
All host systems are themselves embedded in larger systems, creating an infinite regression of containment
Channels through system frontiers (like game controllers or video output) allow information to flow across boundaries in predefined ways, but are often invisible from within
Questions like 'what happened before the Big Bang' are analogous to Mario asking what's beyond the invisible wall—well-formed mathematically but semantically nonsensical
money_quotes: The nothingness beyond the Invisible Boundary of an embedded system is much deeper and more significant than simply not having objects there. In that nothingness there is no programmatic substrate upon which to place objects.
Undefined does not mean 'nothing'; no, no no! It means anything. It is a modern-day Wand of Wonder, a Bag of Tricks, a Horn of Plenty, a Box of Chocolates. You never know what you're going to get.
If a computer program's code inadvertently reaches into Undefined, then it has stepped across a mystical wall into another universe, and anything could happen.
All host systems are embedded in some larger system. This is just the way things work.
Programmers already know intuitively the answer to these questions. The answer is: Undefined is there. Undefined is not the same as 'nothing', no indeed. It's anything. And we can't go that way.
The bugs are so spooky, and so hard to track down, that computers are now designed to be gatekeepers at the Wall.
key_insight: The concept of 'undefined' in programming—where anything can happen when you cross system boundaries—provides a powerful mental model for understanding the inherent limits of asking questions about what lies 'outside' any system, including our universe.
